title: Calligraphy composition and layout
subtitle: Planning margins, hierarchy, and line spacing for a finished calligraphy piece
excerpt: Writing the letters well is one skill; arranging them on a page so the piece reads comfortably and looks balanced is another. Good calligraphy layout planning begins on tracing paper, not on the good sheet. The decisions about margins, text hierarchy, and line breaks are all made before any ink touches the final surface.
sourceNotes: Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906), chapter on layout and margins. The classical margin proportions discussed here derive from Johnston's teaching and from the broader Arts and Crafts calligraphy revival tradition. Internet Archive, archive.org/details/writingilluminat00john.

glossaryTerms.definition: A preparatory version of a calligraphy piece, written at full size on tracing or layout paper to determine line breaks, spacing, and margin proportions before writing on the good sheet.
The visual arrangement of text elements by importance, using size, weight, or colour to distinguish titles, subheadings, and body text.

content.text: A finished calligraphy piece, a quotation, a broadside, a certificate, a letter, has a composition that either works or does not, independent of how well the letterforms are executed. Good letters in a badly planned layout look worse than average letters in a well-planned one. Layout planning is done on tracing or layout paper, in rough, at full size, before any work begins on the good sheet.

The traditional margin proportions for a calligraphy piece on a single sheet are: the top margin is 1 unit; the side margins are 1.5 units each; the bottom margin is 2 units. These are proportions, not absolute measurements. On an A4 sheet with a 3 cm top margin, the side margins are 4.5 cm and the bottom margin is 6 cm.

The deeper bottom margin is counter-intuitive but correct: text placed in the geometric centre of a page looks low. The extra bottom margin compensates for this optical illusion and places the text block in the visual centre.

For prose texts, the line breaks should not split phrases mid-clause if it can be avoided. A line that ends mid-phrase forces the reader to carry meaning across a line break, which interrupts the flow. For verse or quotations, follow the natural phrase breaks of the original text rather than filling each line to the margin.

The right-hand rag, the uneven right edge produced by lines of different lengths, is acceptable in calligraphy. It reads as natural rather than mechanical. Avoid a rag where a very short line is followed by a very long one: this creates a visual hole in the text block.
A piece with more than one element needs a visual

. A simple version: the title in a larger nib (three times the text nib width), the body in the working nib, and the attribution in the same nib as the body but in a lighter colour or at a different angle.

The title and the attribution should not compete in scale. One element dominates, one supports. Avoid having three text elements at three different scales; two scales plus a subtle colour or weight variation is more controlled.

on tracing or layout paper at full size, using the actual nib and ink you will use on the good sheet. This tests not only the visual layout but also the actual letter widths, which vary slightly with humidity and ink consistency. A rough written at half scale often misleads: proportions that look good at half size do not always scale correctly.

Once the rough is approved, place it under the good sheet on the light box to use as a guide for margin and guide-line positioning. Trace the line positions lightly in pencil on the good sheet, then remove the rough and rule the actual calligraphy guide lines.
